Joint feedback and adjustment cycles: Teams met after sessions to review what worked and what didn’t, and co-adjust the next session, improving flow and adaptation over time.
Facilitator flexibility/adaptive delivery: Bloom facilitators who made the script flexible, shared the spotlight, and co-facilitated with UDS staff allowed for smoother integration.
UDS staff flexibility/initiative: UDS educators/nutritionists/pedagogues adapted their own planned activities to complement the Bloom content.
Clear connectors between segments: Intentional transitions or shared activities that linked Bloom segments with UDS components reduced the 'breakdown' and made the session feel complete.
Children’s inclusion in the session: Designing activities where children are the main focus aligned with the norms of the family modality and increased child participation/attendance.
Caregivers-only blocks + second caregiver/childcare: For dense topics, temporarily separating the children (or having a second caregiver) allowed caregivers to focus and reduced stress.
Age-based adaptations (0-2 years): Adapting activities for infants and toddlers made the content viable within the younger age groups of UDS.
Bloom time anchor: Spending 15 minutes with the child provided a simple and memorable thread that integrated well with routines.
Rapport and psychological safety: Warmth and trust-building led to emotional openness and greater engagement.
Time flexibility within 2 hours: Allowing elastic time for transitions/refreshments and mixing didactic elements with play/activity improved adaptation.
Using existing UDS characterization: Drawing on knowledge of family profiles/interests aligned better with casework in course.
Low-load journaling (at home): Positioning the journal primarily as a home tool maintained the session's flow while reinforcing transfer.
Child-friendly space setup: A thoughtful space setup aligned with UDS practice and signaled a distinct and welcoming space.
Family communication channels: Short WhatsApp videos, reminders, and testimonials aligned with existing outreach practices.
Tangible resources to take home: Books, audio/podcasts, or other similar resources reinforced home practice and were appreciated.
Links to local services: Connecting practices with other community spaces extended relevance beyond the UDS setting.
